##   
![](https://sonarsource.github.io/sonarcloud-github-static-resources/v2/common/light_bulb-16px.png '') Catch issues before they fail your Quality Gate with our IDE extension ![](https://sonarsource.github.io/sonarcloud-github-static-resources/v2/common/sonarlint-16px.png '') [SonarQube for IDE](https://www.sonarsource.com/products/sonarlint/features/connected-mode/?referrer=pull-request)

**Third-party release action not pinned to immutable commit SHA** Uses mutable `v2` tag for third-party release action. Pin to a full commit SHA with a version comment.
AI prompt ```text Check if this security scanner issue is valid. If so, understand the root cause and fix it. If appropriate, update or add tests. Keep the change focused and preserve intended behavior. P1 Third-party release action not pinned to immutable commit SHA The release workflow uses `softprops/action-gh-release@v2`, which refers to a mutable tag. If the action repository is compromised, the v2 tag could be force-pushed to point to malicious code that exfiltrates the GITHUB_TOKEN or tampers with release artifacts. Pin the action to a specific 40-character commit SHA and add a version comment, e.g. `uses: softprops/action-gh-release@<sha> # v2.2.0`. ```

**3 issues found** across 10 files
Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues) ```text Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately. P1: Use a full commit SHA for `softprops/action-gh-release` instead of the mutable `v2` tag. Mutable tags can be retargeted to different code, which increases supply-chain risk in this release workflow. P2: Context-menu setup can still hit duplicate-id races on older Chrome builds because `await api.contextMenus.removeAll()` is only reliably awaitable where that API returns a Promise. A compatibility wrapper that waits for either callback completion or Promise resolution would keep the remove-then-create ordering stable. P0: **Chrome will reject the manifest due to `background.scripts`** The manifest.json includes both `"service_worker": "TabExtractor.js"` and `"scripts": ["matcher.js", "TabExtractor.js"]` under the `background` key. Chrome's Manifest V3 schema only accepts `service_worker` (and optional `type: "module"`); the `scripts` array is not valid and causes manifest validation failure. Firefox's MV3 implementation, conversely, does not support `background.service_worker` and uses `background.scripts` instead. `TabExtractor.js` already loads `matcher.js` via `importScripts("matcher.js")` when running in Chrome's service worker context (line 13), so the extension works in Chrome — but it can't be loaded or published because of the invalid manifest property. **Recommendation**: Maintain separate manifest files — `manifest.json` (Chrome, with just `service_worker`) and `manifest.firefox.json` (Firefox, with just `scripts`). The README's release workflow or a simple packaging script can select the right one per target browser. ```
Reply with feedback, questions, or to request a fix.

[Re-trigger cubic](https://www.cubic.dev/action/re-review/pr/hbmartin/chrome-tab-extractor/1/ai_pr_review_1783102080351_7beafbaf-c3d2-49db-bd50-03539d0f3912?returnTo=https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Fhbmartin%2Fchrome-tab-extractor%2Fpull%2F1)

<!-- metadata:{"confidence":9} -->
P0: **Chrome will reject the manifest due to `background.scripts`**

The manifest.json includes both `"service_worker": "TabExtractor.js"` and `"scripts": ["matcher.js", "TabExtractor.js"]` under the `background` key. Chrome's Manifest V3 schema only accepts `service_worker` (and optional `type: "module"`); the `scripts` array is not valid and causes manifest validation failure. Firefox's MV3 implementation, conversely, does not support `background.service_worker` and uses `background.scripts` instead.

`TabExtractor.js` already loads `matcher.js` via `importScripts("matcher.js")` when running in Chrome's service worker context (line 13), so the extension works in Chrome — but it can't be loaded or published because of the invalid manifest property.

**Recommendation**: Maintain separate manifest files — `manifest.json` (Chrome, with just `service_worker`) and `manifest.firefox.json` (Firefox, with just `scripts`). The README's release workflow or a simple packaging script can select the right one per target browser.

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents</summary>

```text
Check if this issue is valid — if so, understand the root cause and fix it. At manifest.json, line 11:

<comment>**Chrome will reject the manifest due to `background.scripts`**

The manifest.json includes both `"service_worker": "TabExtractor.js"` and `"scripts": ["matcher.js", "TabExtractor.js"]` under the `background` key. Chrome's Manifest V3 schema only accepts `service_worker` (and optional `type: "module"`); the `scripts` array is not valid and causes manifest validation failure. Firefox's MV3 implementation, conversely, does not support `background.service_worker` and uses `background.scripts` instead.

`TabExtractor.js` already loads `matcher.js` via `importScripts("matcher.js")` when running in Chrome's service worker context (line 13), so the extension works in Chrome — but it can't be loaded or published because of the invalid manifest property.

**Recommendation**: Maintain separate manifest files — `manifest.json` (Chrome, with just `service_worker`) and `manifest.firefox.json` (Firefox, with just `scripts`). The README's release workflow or a simple packaging script can select the right one per target browser.</comment>

-        "service_worker": "TabExtractor.js"
+        "service_worker": "TabExtractor.js",
+        "scripts": ["matcher.js", "TabExtractor.js"]
     },
     "icons": {
</file context>
```

P2: Context-menu setup can still hit duplicate-id races on older Chrome builds because `await api.contextMenus.removeAll()` is only reliably awaitable where that API returns a Promise. A compatibility wrapper that waits for either callback completion or Promise resolution would keep the remove-then-create ordering stable.

<details>

A compatibility wrapper that waits for either callback completion or Promise resolution would keep the remove-then-create ordering stable.</comment>

**Third-party release action not pinned to immutable commit SHA** Uses mutable `v2` tag for third-party release action. Pin to a full commit SHA with a version comment.
AI prompt ```text Check if this security scanner issue is valid. If so, understand the root cause and fix it. If appropriate, update or add tests. Keep the change focused and preserve intended behavior. P1 Third-party release action not pinned to immutable commit SHA The release workflow uses `softprops/action-gh-release@v2`, which refers to a mutable tag. If the action repository is compromised, the v2 tag could be force-pushed to point to malicious code that exfiltrates the GITHUB_TOKEN or tampers with release artifacts. Pin the action to a specific 40-character commit SHA and add a version comment, e.g. `uses: softprops/action-gh-release@<sha> # v2.2.0`. ```

P0: **Chrome will reject the manifest due to `background.scripts`**

The manifest.json includes both `"service_worker": "TabExtractor.js"` and `"scripts": ["matcher.js", "TabExtractor.js"]` under the `background` key. Chrome's Manifest V3 schema only accepts `service_worker` (and optional `type: "module"`); the `scripts` array is not valid and causes manifest validation failure. Firefox's MV3 implementation, conversely, does not support `background.service_worker` and uses `background.scripts` instead.

`TabExtractor.js` already loads `matcher.js` via `importScripts("matcher.js")` when running in Chrome's service worker context (line 13), so the extension works in Chrome — but it can't be loaded or published because of the invalid manifest property.

**Recommendation**: Maintain separate manifest files — `manifest.json` (Chrome, with just `service_worker`) and `manifest.firefox.json` (Firefox, with just `scripts`). The README's release workflow or a simple packaging script can select the right one per target browser.
